responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity infrastructure faces a critical supplier-concentration risk: MitID's authentication model depends structurally on Apple and Google's platform integrity signals, app distribution controls, and device telemetry. This creates a resilience and sovereignty problem that cannot be solved by retrofitting MitID itself—the system is too conservative and tightly coupled. The project intervenes upstream through AltID procurement, standards-setting, and credible technical demonstration of platform-neutral alternatives. The core tension is between the convenience and security guarantees of proprietary platforms versus the strategic autonomy and continuity resilience of open standards. Success requires balancing three competing forces: (1) technical credibility sufficient to shift procurement language without overcommitting to production readiness; (2) coalition-building across civil society, privacy advocates, and policy-makers to overcome incumbent inertia; and (3) EU-level standards alignment to avoid isolated Danish action. The selected levers address the primary decision points that determine whether platform-neutral access becomes institutionalized before the next identity stack hardens existing dependencies.

levers.name: Feasibility Report Framing and Institutional Positioning
levers.consequences: Immediate: A published feasibility and risk report reframes platform dependency as a resilience and supplier-concentration issue rather than a purely technical or ideological concern, shifting bureaucratic attention from feature parity to contingency-planning risk. Systemic: Framing dependency as a public-sector continuity problem increases likelihood that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or AltID stakeholders formally acknowledge the risk in procurement language by 15–25%, because risk articulation is harder for institutional actors to dismiss than privacy advocacy alone. Strategic: A credible, institution-friendly risk narrative that decouples platform-neutral requirements from activist framing enables procurement teams to justify supplier-diversity language to finance and security committees without appearing to challenge Apple and Google directly.
options: Commission an independent feasibility report through a recognized Danish research institution that emphasizes supplier concentration, contingency resilience, and procurement neutrality, explicitly avoiding ideological positioning and instead anchoring the argument in public-sector business continuity and regulatory compliance under eIDAS2.

Conduct an internal Digitaliseringsstyrelsen-commissioned risk audit on MitID's platform dependencies, framed as a mandatory suppliers-risk assessment aligned with EU procurement and resilience directives, positioning the study as routine compliance work rather than external pressure.

Develop a multi-stakeholder white paper jointly authored by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the Danish Financial Supervisory Authority, and an academic security lab, presenting platform-neutral fallback access as a regulatory requirement under proposed Nordic digital identity standards, establishing shared institutional ownership.
levers.review_lever: Controls technical credibility vs. institutional buy-in. Weakness: The options do not account for timing friction—Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may commission studies but deprioritize them unless tied explicitly to an immediate procurement or regulatory deadline.

levers.name: Demonstrator Scope and Evidence Weight
levers.consequences: Immediate: Two lean non-production demonstrators (mobile-based FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS, plus a browser-or-token-based fallback path) provide technical proof that standards-based, platform-neutral access is feasible without requiring Apple or Google as trust anchors. Systemic: Working demonstrators reduce perceived technical risk by 30–40% among procurement teams and EU standards bodies, because visual, hands-on evidence shifts confidence away from 'theoretically possible' toward 'operationally demonstrated,' even when demonstrators are not production-certified. Strategic: Demonstrators create a credible artifact that justifies inclusion of platform-neutral and fallback-access language in AltID procurement documents without forcing Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to build or certify an alternative identity system themselves.
options: Build a tightly scoped mobile demonstrator using GrapheneOS and open-source FIDO2 server infrastructure that authenticates only against a non-production Danish identity-provider simulation, explicitly documented as a proof-of-concept with no integration path to live MitID or AltID systems.

Develop a browser-based and hardware-token fallback authenticator (supporting FIDO2 USB keys and WebAuthn) that works across iOS and Android without requiring app-store distribution, paired with a minimal simulated public-sector identity-provider, demonstrating that secure authentication can operate outside platform app-distribution channels.

Create a modular reference implementation library in Go and TypeScript that third-party developers can use to prototype platform-neutral authentication patterns, released under open-source governance with explicit non-production warnings, enabling external security researchers and procurement teams to test and adapt the design without direct project involvement.
levers.review_lever: Controls technical scope vs. institutional legitimacy. Weakness: The options underestimate the time required for security vetting and stakeholder sign-off on demonstrators; a 'reference implementation' risks becoming a de facto standard before any independent review, creating reputational exposure.

levers.name: Coalition Leadership and Stakeholder Lock-In
levers.consequences: Immediate: A formal coalition spanning privacy advocates, academic security researchers, civil-society organizations, contingency-planning experts, and sympathetic Folketinget members amplifies the project's voice beyond a single research team and distributes reputational risk across multiple credible institutions. Systemic: Coalition engagement increases the probability that at least one formal institutional stakeholder (academic institution, privacy watchdog, or parliamentary committee member) publishes or co-authors a position paper on platform-neutral access by month 12, creating independent pressure that reduces reliance on the project itself for narrative coherence. Strategic: A multi-stakeholder coalition makes it politically costly for Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to ignore platform-neutrality language, because dismissing the proposal now requires public rebuttal to multiple respected institutions rather than a single external project.
options: Establish a formal advisory board with rotating leadership from the Danish Data Protection Authority, IT-Politisk Forening, and Cøbenhavn Kommune's digital innovation office, meeting monthly and publishing position statements on platform dependency, creating institutional entrenchment and shared governance.

Recruit and financially support independent peer-review by international experts (from EU digital-identity working groups, BSI Germany, Swedish eID authorities) to publicly validate feasibility findings, generating third-party institutional endorsement that strengthens credibility with Danish authorities.

Organize a series of on-the-record workshops and policy forums with Finanstilsynet, Ministry of Health, Danish National Police, and the Danish Agency for Data Supply, positioning platform-neutral authentication as a cross-sector resilience requirement for banking, healthcare, and law enforcement, not just a general-purpose public-sector issue.
levers.review_lever: Controls coalition autonomy vs. institutional alignment. Weakness: The options do not address the risk that coalition members may fragment or deprioritize platform-neutrality work once they face competing institutional demands, particularly if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen signals low immediate priority.

levers.name: Procurement Language and AltID Intervention Timing
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project submits formally drafted procurement requirement language (platform-neutrality, fallback-access, supplier-concentration, continuity-planning clauses) to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the AltID steering committee before month 16, embedding the language directly into official requirements documentation rather than leaving it as a recommendation. Systemic: If procurement language reaches final AltID tender documents, adoption likelihood for at least one platform-neutral or fallback requirement increases by 50–70%, because procurement teams follow explicit written requirements far more reliably than general advocacy; conversely, if procurement deadlines pass before language is formally submitted, the intervention window closes for 3–5 years. Strategic: Embedding platform-neutrality language in procurement documents institutionalizes the requirement regardless of political or vendor pushback, because procurement compliance becomes mandatory for any bidder, shifting the default from 'optional innovation' to 'contractual obligation.'
options: Submit formally drafted procurement amendments to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's AltID procurement working group by month 14, specifying that any AltID implementation must include a certified fallback authentication path independent of proprietary app-store distribution, with detailed compliance metrics and acceptance criteria.

Engage directly with the AltID technical steering committee to propose a formal 'resilience and contingency' procurement track that runs parallel to the main architecture workstream, focusing on fallback and platform-neutral authentication as a separate contractual requirement and evaluation criterion.

Wait until the official AltID Request for Proposal (RFP) public comment period and submit detailed technical comments and requirement proposals through formal public-procurement channels, ensuring that feedback is documented in the official record and requires a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
levers.review_lever: Controls intervention timing vs. procurement lock-in risk. Weakness: The options do not account for the possibility that AltID procurement timelines may shift, procurement governance may change hands, or formal comment periods may open and close without adequate notice to the project.

levers.name: EU Standards Alignment and Extraterritorial Leverage
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project engages with European Digital Identity Wallet working groups and eIDAS2 standards bodies to advocate for platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms in EU-wide digital identity materials, positioning Denmark as a leader in interoperability and resilience rather than a lone voice. Systemic: If language on platform-neutrality or fallback access appears in official EU Digital Identity Wallet guidance by month 20, Danish procurement teams face 25–40% increased pressure to adopt similar language, because EU directives and soft-law guidance create compliance expectations that are difficult for individual member states to ignore. Strategic: EU-level standards alignment converts platform-neutrality from a Danish procurement issue into a pan-European interoperability requirement, leveraging regulatory momentum to shift vendor and incumbent behavior across multiple markets simultaneously.
options: Designate a project team member to represent Denmark in the eIDAS2 technical implementation working groups, presenting demonstrator findings and platform-neutrality requirements as contributions to the Wallet's interoperability and resilience tracks, formally submitting technical and procurement input by month 18.

Coordinate with Sweden's eID platform (BankID, Freja) and German sovereignty initiatives (BSI, gematik) to jointly publish a position paper on platform-neutral digital identity requirements, framing it as a Nordic–German resilience coalition and submitting it to relevant EU bodies and the European Commission's digital infrastructure task force.

Commission an external EU-based policy institute (such as the Centre for European Policy Studies or a relevant technical standards organization) to produce a white paper on platform dependence in European digital identity systems, releasing it through EU channels to establish an independent institutional narrative that Danish efforts can reference and amplify.
levers.review_lever: Controls EU regulatory leverage vs. Danish sovereignty autonomy. Weakness: The options assume EU bodies will prioritize platform-neutrality concerns within existing Digital Wallet timelines; in practice, EU standards bodies often defer to market-driven approaches and move slowly on sovereignty issues.

levers.name: Regulatory Approval Pathway and Compliance Framework
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project maps and formally engages with the specific regulatory approval gates and decision-makers required for platform-neutral authentication to be recognized as compliant under eIDAS2 Substantial level, including NSIS, MitID certification bodies, and AltID's technical governance. Systemic: Securing formal written guidance from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the Danish eIDAS2 compliance authority on how platform-neutral authenticators qualify for 'Substantial' level certification by month 14 reduces regulatory uncertainty by 35–50%, because bureaucratic clarity reduces the perception of legal risk and makes procurement teams more willing to accept non-proprietary designs. Strategic: A formal certification pathway for platform-neutral fallback authenticators removes the structural argument that 'only Apple and Google can meet regulatory requirements,' shifting the debate from 'Is this even legal?' to 'Which vendor best implements the legal standard?'
options: Initiate a formal dialogue with the Danish eIDAS2 competent authority and MitID's certification body to clarify how GrapheneOS-based or hardware-token authenticators qualify for Substantial level assurance, requesting written guidance on compliance pathways and approval timelines before month 12.

Propose a formal 'Platform-Neutral Authenticator' certification scheme as an amendment to Danish NSIS or AltID governance documents, specifying technical, security, and resilience requirements that any platform-independent design must meet, creating a formal institutional track rather than relying on ad-hoc compliance interpretation.

Engage with relevant EU notified bodies (NIST, BSI, or equivalent EU regulatory bodies) to establish a pilot interoperability program for platform-neutral authenticators under eIDAS2, positioning Denmark as a test jurisdiction and creating formal regulatory precedent across Europe.
levers.review_lever: Controls regulatory clarity vs. incumbent gatekeeping. Weakness: The options do not account for the possibility that existing regulatory bodies may be reluctant to establish new certification pathways, perceiving them as undermining established MitID or Apple/Google compliance models.

levers.name: Contingency Planning and Geopolitical Risk Framing
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project explicitly frames platform-neutral access as a contingency and resilience measure tied to potential geopolitical disruptions, supply-chain vulnerabilities, or regulatory changes affecting US technology companies (e.g., TikTok bans, sanctions, or digital sovereignty laws), reframing the issue away from ideology toward risk management. Systemic: Contingency framing increases institutional receptiveness by 20–35%, because public-sector organizations are more willing to fund resilience measures for low-probability but high-impact scenarios than to challenge existing vendors on principle; moreover, if external geopolitical events occur (US export controls, Apple policy changes), contingency language that was previously marginalized suddenly becomes immediately relevant. Strategic: A formally documented contingency plan tied to platform-neutral access ensures that if MitID or AltID dependencies become exposed through policy change or supply disruption, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has a pre-established fallback architecture and procurement roadmap, rather than scrambling to build an alternative under crisis conditions.
options: Develop a formal 'Digital Identity Continuity-of-Service Plan' for Digitaliseringsstyrelsen outlining specific failure scenarios (Apple/Google policy change, US sanctions, platform availability disruption) and mapped fallback architectures, submitted by month 12 as a risk-management document rather than a strategic proposal.

Commission a geopolitical and supply-chain risk assessment from an independent security or strategic consultancy, explicitly analyzing the exposure of Danish critical identity infrastructure to US technology policy, export controls, and platform-vendor policy changes, framing the work as mandatory due diligence.

Establish a formal 'Digital Identity Resilience Steering Committee' within the Ministry of Digitization with representatives from the Finance Ministry, Defense, and Foreign Service, tasked with reviewing supplier concentration risk across critical infrastructure and formally endorsing platform-neutral fallback requirements as a defense-policy matter, not merely a technical or convenience issue.
levers.review_lever: Controls external risk framing vs. institutional panic resistance. Weakness: The options may inadvertently trigger defensive responses from incumbent vendors or create the perception of anti-US sentiment, potentially delegitimizing the project if contingency framing is perceived as alarmist rather than evidence-based.

responses.summary: The lever set is well-targeted at the project's core objective—shifting from technical disruption toward procurement and standards intervention—but contains two critical imbalances. First, Levers 1 and 3 (feasibility framing and coalition-building) risk conflating narrow Danish procurement impact with broad institutional change; the project may succeed in publishing credible work and securing meetings without translating that into formal procurement language. Second, Lever 4 (procurement intervention) is the highest-leverage point but carries timing risk: if AltID procurement schedules shift or deadlines close before formal submissions, the intervention window collapses and the project defaults to EU-facing work with uncertain ROI. The levers assume constructive engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, but the plan explicitly warns against expecting cooperation from existing operators; Lever 6 (regulatory approval) should be strengthened by identifying specific regulatory blockers and preparing alternative certification pathways if the primary eIDAS2 authority proves uncooperative. Add to Lever 4: 'Develop contingent

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity system faces a structural resilience crisis: critical public services depend on two foreign platform providers whose integrity APIs, distribution policies, and geopolitical alignment cannot be controlled by Danish authorities. The seven levers below navigate the core tension between maintaining immediate MitID stability (requiring vendor cooperation and regulatory conservatism) and creating genuine platform-neutral alternatives (requiring technical credibility, procurement leverage, and EU-level coordination). The levers are sequenced to build evidence and coalition strength before attempting formal intervention in AltID design and procurement, ensuring that by month 24, Denmark possesses both technical proof-of-concept and institutional momentum to embed supplier-neutral requirements into the next identity stack generation.

levers.name: Technical Credibility Threshold and Demonstrator Ambition Calibration
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deciding whether demonstrators prove feasibility through high-fidelity simulation of live MitID endpoints or through abstract proof-of-concept on sandboxed environments determines the credibility weight the project carries into phase three. → Systemic: High-fidelity demonstrators require 6–8 additional weeks of development and 15–20% budget reallocation, but increase institutional confidence in fallback paths by an estimated 40–50% based on peer review feedback; low-fidelity approaches preserve timeline but risk being dismissed as 'not real-world relevant' by procurement decision-makers. → Strategic: This choice determines whether the project's technical output functions as a binding constraint on AltID design discussions or remains advisory commentary that can be deferred without political cost.
options: Develop demonstrators using live MitID staging environments and actual GrapheneOS device hardware, with formal security review by an independent third party before public release, to establish maximum institutional credibility even if timeline slips to month 20.

Build functionally realistic but architecturally isolated demonstrators that simulate MitID and eIDAS2 endpoints using open-source reference implementations, with security review limited to internal team and invited academic reviewers, balancing credibility against schedule.

Create proof-of-concept prototypes using simplified authentication flows and synthetic identity-provider environments, publish methodology and limitations transparently, and focus phase-three leverage on standards input rather than technical validation.
levers.review_lever: Controls technical purity vs. institutional influence. Weakness: The options do not adequately address the risk that demonstrator complexity may alienate non-technical procurement stakeholders, nor do they specify how security review findings will be communicated to decision-makers in accessible language.

levers.name: Coalition Composition and Civil-Society Legitimacy Anchoring
levers.consequences: Immediate: Recruiting specific civil-society organisations, academic institutions, and parliamentary advocates establishes the project as a public-interest initiative rather than a vendor-neutral technical exercise. → Systemic: Including organizations with established credibility on privacy (e.g., Dansk Folkepartis digital-rights caucus, university security labs) increases the probability that phase-one findings receive parliamentary attention and media coverage by an estimated 30–35%, while also introducing competing priorities and slower decision cycles (±3–4 weeks in consensus-building). → Strategic: Early coalition lock-in shapes what language (resilience vs. privacy vs. sovereignty) dominates the public narrative, influencing whether AltID procurement teams frame platform neutrality as a security imperative or as activist pressure to be managed.
options: Invite 2–3 established civil-society organisations with existing parliamentary relationships (e.g., human-rights groups, digital-rights advocates) to co-author the phase-one feasibility report and formally endorse the project's framing, accepting slower decision cycles but gaining institutional legitimacy.

Partner selectively with academic researchers and one sympathetic Folketinget member to co-sign policy proposals, limiting coalition overhead while maintaining plausible independence from government capture.

Build coalition reactively during phase one by publishing feasibility findings first and then inviting external stakeholders to validate conclusions, preserving project agility but risking that narrative framing is already set by institutional actors.
levers.review_lever: Controls insider legitimacy vs. grassroots momentum. Weakness: The options assume that civil-society partners will prioritize platform neutrality equally with privacy or affordability; they do not account for potential coalition fragmentation if partner organizations prioritize conflicting agendas (e.g., anti-tech regulation vs. digital-access equity).

levers.name: Regulatory Framing Architecture and Risk Narrative Construction
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project must decide whether to emphasize MitID's dependency as a supplier-concentration risk (procurement/resilience frame), a democratic-accountability issue (governance frame), or a privacy/data-minimization concern (civil-liberties frame). → Systemic: Resilience framing increases traction with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and procurement teams by ~25%, but requires quantifying continuity-of-service costs; governance framing resonates with parliamentarians but invites Teliadenmark and other incumbents to reframe as populist critique of innovation; privacy framing activates civil society but may alienate technology-optimist factions within government. Each frame carries ~2–3 week delays in stakeholder alignment because different audiences prioritize different evidence types. → Strategic: The selected narrative becomes the lens through which all subsequent demonstrator results and policy proposals are interpreted; misalignment between the project's framing and key decision-makers' preexisting priorities compounds institutional friction by month 20–24.
options: Anchor the entire project narrative on quantified continuity-of-service and resilience risk, producing a formal supplier-concentration audit showing the estimated cost-impact of a six-month iOS or Android policy change affecting MitID distribution, to align directly with public-sector enterprise-risk frameworks.

Present platform dependency as a democratic-sovereignty issue tied to Denmark's constitutional safeguards and public-sector autonomy, emphasizing that critical government services should not depend on commercial platform policies, and frame fallback paths as a constitutional resilience requirement.

Construct a three-layered narrative (resilience + governance + privacy) where each audience segment receives customized messaging, accepting the risk of perceived inconsistency but maximizing relevance across fragmented decision-making bodies and stakeholder camps.
levers.review_lever: Controls institutional risk language vs. sovereignty rhetoric. Weakness: The options do not address the possibility that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may perceive any framing emphasizing platform dependency as implicit criticism of MitID's current design and respond defensively rather than engaging constructively with the project's proposals.

levers.name: EU Coordination Timing and Standards Influence Sequencing
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can engage EU-level eIDAS2 and European Digital Identity Wallet working groups either concurrently with Danish phase-two work or sequentially after Danish demonstrators are complete. → Systemic: Concurrent engagement (starting month 6) amplifies the project's voice in emerging European standards (~20% increase in language inclusion probability based on timing of EC consultation cycles) but distributes team attention and requires bilingual policy communication; sequential engagement (starting month 17) preserves focus on Danish institutional traction but risks that EU requirements have already been finalized without Danish input, and that phase-three leverage with AltID is weakened by the absence of EU-level momentum. → Strategic: EU-level adoption of platform-neutrality language creates a binding constraint on Danish AltID procurement (because eIDAS2 compliance is mandatory), whereas purely Danish advocacy can be deferred or reinterpreted by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
options: Engage the European Digital Identity Wallet working groups starting month 4 by contributing a Danish use-case paper on platform-neutral fallback paths, positioning the project as an early input to European standards architecture and influencing EC policy language before formal working drafts are locked.

Delay EU engagement until month 18, after demonstrators are complete and phase-three Danish leverage is clear, ensuring that the project's EU contribution is grounded in credible technical evidence and that Danish procurement progress can be leveraged to influence European alignment.

Establish a lightweight EU liaison role (0.3 FTE from month 6) that attends key working group meetings and informal EC consultations without committing the full project team, balancing visibility against resource constraints while maintaining optionality to escalate engagement.
levers.review_lever: Controls early European influence vs. downstream institutional leverage. Weakness: The options underestimate the political difficulty of shifting European Digital Identity Wallet design at the working-group stage; they assume that technical input from a small Danish project will move EC policy, when in fact EC priorities are already set by member-state governments and incumbent industry coalitions.

levers.name: Demonstrator Independence and Open-Source Licensing Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can release demonstrator code under a permissive open-source license (MIT, Apache 2.0), a copyleft license (GPL v3), or under restricted institutional access with deferred public release. → Systemic: Permissive licensing accelerates adoption by downstream projects and vendors by ~40%, but relinquishes the project's narrative control and creates risk that demonstrator code is used by platforms to claim false compliance; copyleft licensing preserves some control and signals commitment to open standards, but reduces adoption by commercial entities by ~25–30% and may invite legal scrutiny from patent holders; restricted access protects institutional control and allows selective distribution for credibility building, but weakens the public-interest framing and invites accusations of gatekeeping. → Strategic: The licensing choice determines whether the project's technical work becomes a public good (enabling downstream resilience research) or an institutional asset (enabling the project to maintain influence over interpretation and deployment).
options: Publish all demonstrator code and architecture under GPL v3, requiring that derivative systems remain open-source and documenting the licensing choice as a statement of commitment to digital-commons principles and non-commercial resilience, accepting reduced vendor adoption as a trade-off for ecosystem integrity.

Release demonstrators under a permissive MIT license with explicit documentation of intended use (research and institutional validation only) and a formal statement that commercial deployment must undergo independent security audit, balancing openness with accountability.

Maintain demonstrator source code under institutional access (available to authorized academic and government partners under research agreements), publish detailed architecture and methodology publicly, and commit to full open-source release following formal European Digital Identity Wallet standards finalization, preserving leverage during the critical phase-three window.
levers.review_lever: Controls ecosystem enablement vs. institutional positioning. Weakness: The options do not adequately address the tension between claiming that platform-neutral authentication is a public good (requiring open access) and maintaining sufficient project control to ensure that demonstrators are not misused by incumbents to create false-compliance narratives or proprietary alternatives.

levers.name: Formal Authority Engagement Strategy and Institutional Capture Boundaries
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project must define the depth of operational engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the MitID operator—ranging from advisory consultation (information-sharing, no decision influence) to co-design partnership (shared research agenda, steering-committee seats). → Systemic: Deep operational engagement (steering committee, formal input into AltID architecture) increases the probability of procurement-language inclusion by ~35% and accelerates institutional decision cycles by 8–12 weeks, but creates risk that the project becomes perceived as a government arm and loses civil-society coalition support; conversely, purely advisory engagement preserves independence and coalition credibility, but reduces institutional traction and increases the likelihood that phase-three procurement language is ignored as 'external input' without binding weight. → Strategic: The engagement model chosen determines whether the project functions as an independent credibility source influencing procurement from outside or as an insider constrained by institutional politics and incumbent resistance.
options: Establish a formal joint steering committee with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the MitID operator beginning month 3, with the project team providing research input directly into AltID architecture discussions and accepting governance-sharing obligations that may delay some decisions but embed platform-neutrality requirements into procurement planning.

Engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen through structured quarterly consultations and formal comment periods on public procurement drafts, maintaining arm's-length independence while ensuring that the project's findings reach decision-makers in documented form that creates institutional accountability.

Conduct engagement through informal meetings with sympathetic contacts within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and publish findings independently, maximizing the project's freedom to critique and propose alternatives while accepting that institutional influence depends on sustained media and parliamentary pressure rather than formal channels.
levers.review_lever: Controls institutional integration vs. analytical independence. Weakness: The options do not address the likelihood that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen will view the project as either a compliance threat (if too independent) or as administrative overhead (if too integrated), and that the project's ability to influence procurement depends less on the chosen engagement model than on whether top political decision-makers have already committed to platform neutrality as a policy priority.

levers.name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Architecture and Technological Diversity Forcing
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project must select whether to develop a single fallback pathway (e.g., browser-based FIDO2 only) or to demonstrate multiple architecturally distinct fallback routes (browser-based, hardware-token, SMS-backed, distributed identity) to show that no single foreign platform is a technical necessity. → Systemic: A single pathway is defensible, lean, and completable within phase-two budget (~3.0 million DKK), but a procurement reviewer can argue 'this is only one option—not a systemic alternative'; multiple pathways require 4.5–5.0 million DKK (20% budget increase) and 4–6 additional weeks of integration testing, but create a more robust innovation signal and reduce the argument that fallback authentication is a niche use case. The presence of two or three distinct working pathways increases the estimated probability of AltID procurement language including 'support for multiple authentication methods' by ~30–40%. → Strategic: Multiple pathways establish that platform-neutral resilience is not a technical constraint or an ideological luxury, but a practical engineering problem with multiple solutions—fundamentally shifting the burden of proof onto incumbents to argue why diversity should be rejected.
options: Develop two distinct demonstrators in parallel: one mobile-native FIDO2/WebAuthn system on privacy-respecting Android and one browser-based authentication using standard-compliant OpenID Connect with optional hardware-token support, showing that resilience does not require proprietary platform dependency.

Create a single authoritative fallback pathway using browser-based authentication with explicit hardware-token integration, supported by a detailed technical specification showing how this approach scales to national-level deployment without requiring changes to MitID's current app-based infrastructure.

Develop a modular authentication framework that demonstrates three distinct fallback pathways (browser-based, SMS-backed OTP, hardware-token) integrated into a single codebase, allowing different public-sector agencies to select pathways matching their citizen accessibility and security requirements without lock-in to any single authentication method.
levers.review_lever: Controls simplicity vs. systemic resilience demonstration. Weakness: The options assume that multiple demonstrators necessarily increase institutional confidence, when in fact overambition in scope can result in incomplete implementations that reduce credibility more than a single, well-executed fallback pathway would achieve.

responses.summary: The seven levers are well-targeted on the core institutional and technical barriers: they address technical credibility, coalition legitimacy, narrative framing, EU alignment, licensing strategy, authority engagement, and architectural diversity. However, they contain one critical missing dimension: **financing continuity risk and phase-gating enforcement**. The plan's phase-gating structure (phase two conditional on phase-one deliverables and institutional engagement) is sound in principle, but the levers do not address what happens if phase-one fails to secure formal written institutional engagement or if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen blocks phase-two funding. This creates a planning vacuum where the project could reach month 9 with demonstrators funded but with no clear procurement traction. **Add to Lever 6 (Institutional Engagement) an explicit option:** 'Establish a contingency funding and scope-reduction protocol whereby if formal Digitaliseringsstyrelsen engagement is not secured by month 8, the project reduces phase-two demonstrator scope by 30% and reallocates savings to EU standards engagement and coalition publication work, ensuring that the project delivers value even if Danish institutional traction stalls.' This makes the project's resilience explicit and prevents sunk-cost escalation.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity architecture faces a structural resilience crisis masked by functional adequacy: MitID's dependence on Apple/Google platform signals creates a single point of failure disguised as multi-vendor choice. The project's core tension is between **institutional inertia** (MitID operators and public sector prefer proven, certified incumbents) and **strategic urgency** (eIDAS2/AltID rollout represents a narrow window to encode platform neutrality before the next stack hardens). The selected levers prioritize **procurement language design**, **demonstrator credibility calibration**, **coalition institutional anchoring**, **geopolitical risk framing**, and **technical standards sequencing** because these directly control whether AltID and related governance bodies encode supplier diversity as a hard requirement rather than a post-hoc consideration. Speed-focused levers are rejected; instead, credibility and institutional embedding dominate.

levers.name: Demonstrator Technical Risk Positioning and Certification Pathway Selection
levers.consequences: Immediate: Choosing between lightweight proof-of-concept (GrapheneOS WebAuthn) versus production-grade hardware-backed fallback (FIDO2 + secure enclave simulation) determines whether reviewers treat the work as exploratory or as evidence of viability. Systemic: A low-certification-threshold demonstrator may reach stakeholders 6 months faster but risks dismissal as 'not production-relevant'; a high-certification-level build (80% toward formal audit readiness) delays phase-two delivery by 3–4 months but anchors claims that platform-neutral access meets 'Substantial' assurance levels—a 40–50% improvement in procurement credibility with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen. Strategic: This trade-off determines whether the project influences AltID procurement language as a credible technical peer or as an academic thought experiment.
options: Build a lightweight GrapheneOS + WebAuthn integration that proves concept feasibility within 6 months and accepts that production-grade certification is left to future operators.

Develop demonstrators with formal security review (third-party vulnerability assessment and compliance gap analysis) targeting 'Substantial' assurance compliance, accepting 3–4 month schedule friction and 400–600k DKK additional review budget.

Deliver two parallel demonstrators—one rapid proof-of-concept for stakeholder communication and a second higher-assurance build targeting formal evaluation frameworks—requiring split technical team and staged phase-two funding release.
levers.review_lever: Controls demonstrator ambition vs. implementation velocity. Weakness: The options do not account for the institutional politics of certification: even high-assurance work may be dismissed if the certifying body (e.g., a MitID operator or legacy regulator) views it as competitive threat rather than evidence.

levers.name: AltID Engagement Timing and Procurement Document Infiltration
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can attempt 'early infiltration' by submitting written input during AltID's design or pre-procurement consultation phases (months 10–14), or 'late intervention' by targeting the formal procurement tender language itself (months 18–22). Systemic: Early engagement provides 8 months of potential design influence but depends on voluntary receptiveness; formal tender involvement guarantees visibility to procurement officers but may trigger incumbent pushback and compliance objections, adding 2–3 months of negotiation and 200–300k DKK in legal/procurement support costs. Strategic: Timing misalignment (too early = ignored, too late = entrenched resistance) determines whether platform-neutrality requirements appear as genuine design principles or as add-on clauses that vendors can legally sidestep.
options: Initiate informal engagement with AltID program management during months 9–12 by offering feasibility findings as 'best practice input,' building trust before formal procurement, and hoping voluntary alignment will emerge.

Target the formal procurement Request for Proposals (RFP) window explicitly by submitting technical requirements language as a formal stakeholder input, accepting that this surfaces the intervention as adversarial and may trigger defensive institutional responses.

Dual-track by embedding a sympathetic Digitaliseringsstyrelsen advisor or consultancy partner into early AltID working groups while separately preparing formal RFP comments, creating internal pressure for neutrality before external procurement debate occurs.
levers.review_lever: Controls stakeholder relationship depth vs. formal procurement leverage. Weakness: The options assume AltID procurement gates and timelines are publicly known and fluid; in reality, procurement schedules often lock in months before public announcement, making timing predictions speculative.

levers.name: Coalition Institutional Embedding and Continuity-Planning Authority Leverage
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can anchor coalition membership in existing Danish institutions (university digital-governance centers, cybersecurity agencies, privacy regulators) or in new informal networks (ad-hoc working groups, civil-society consortia). Systemic: Institutionally embedded coalitions gain standing to comment on public procurement and policy consultations, raising coalition voice-amplification by 60–80% in official channels; informal networks are more agile but lack formal consultation standing and depend entirely on individual advocacy effort, reducing sustained institutional pressure by 40–50% over the 24-month project horizon. Strategic: The choice between institutional-rootedness and coalition flexibility determines whether the project's advocacy survives leadership or personnel transitions, and whether it can claim legitimate standing to influence critical-infrastructure procurement.
options: Formalize coalition participation through a steering committee hosted at a Danish university or independent research center, with official membership status for participating organizations, creating institutional permanence at cost of slower decision-making and organizational overhead.

Build an informal, fluid coalition of individual experts, researchers, and advocates who coordinate via regular working groups and social channels, maximizing agility and intellectual freedom but depending entirely on volunteer continuity and lacking formal consultation standing.

Create a hybrid structure in which a core formal steering committee (3–4 institutional anchors) is paired with an open advisory network of individual contributors, allowing official standing while preserving coalition flexibility and reducing organizational drag.
levers.review_lever: Controls institutional credibility vs. coalition agility. Weakness: The options do not specify how to prevent institutional anchor-organizations from prioritizing their own risk-mitigation over the project's resilience advocacy, a common failure mode when formal governance adds bureaucratic caution.

levers.name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Architecture and Device-Lock Prevention
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project must choose between designing fallback systems that **assume continued mobile OS availability but reduce platform-dependency** (e.g., cross-platform WebAuthn on any OS) versus **eliminating mobile-OS requirement entirely** (browser-only, hardware tokens, or trusted-hardware-free server-side federation). Systemic: A mobile-resilient approach (reducing but not eliminating phone OS dependency) can leverage existing Android/iOS ecosystem and is familiar to certification bodies, adding 3–6 months to time-to-credibility; a fully device-lock-free design is architecturally cleaner but requires novel certification frameworks and invites 8–12 month regulatory debate over new assurance models, increasing phase-three engagement complexity by 50–70%. Strategic: This choice determines whether fallback authentication is framed as a near-term regulatory fix or as a long-term architectural redesign, affecting procurement language, certification pathways, and vendor resistance trajectories.
options: Design fallback authentication using browser-based OpenID Connect with server-side risk assessment (no app requirement), accepting that browser-only access may be perceived as weaker assurance and requires novel regulatory framing for public-sector authentication.

Develop cross-platform WebAuthn fallbacks deployable on any Android distribution (LineageOS, GrapheneOS, de-Googled variants) and FIDO2 hardware tokens, maintaining familiar mobile-app paradigm while reducing exclusive dependence on commercial platform ecosystems.

Pursue a hybrid server-side federation model (passwordless federation with risk-adaptive authentication) that eliminates device-lock-in entirely but requires substantial regulatory reframing and certification effort, delaying phase-three procurement influence by 6–9 months.
levers.review_lever: Controls architectural purism vs. regulatory pragmatism. Weakness: The options assume fallback designs can be tested credibly without access to actual MitID backend systems or production certification infrastructure, leaving regulatory viability of any fallback pathway unvalidated until phase three.

levers.name: Geopolitical Risk Framing and Supply-Chain Continuity Narrative
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can frame platform dependency as a resilience/continuity-planning issue (focusing on supplier concentration and service-continuity risk), as a geopolitical autonomy issue (focusing on US-China tech competition and European digital sovereignty), or as a privacy/democratic-control issue (focusing on data minimization and citizen agency). Systemic: Resilience framing is most likely to resonate with risk-averse public-sector procurement officers and is defensible in EU regulation (estimated 50–60% institutional receptiveness); geopolitical framing is politically salient and aligns with EU strategic autonomy language, but triggers trade-policy scrutiny and risks being dismissed as protectionist, reducing institutional receptiveness to 30–40%; privacy framing is philosophically coherent but historically underweights procurement decisions (15–20% institutional leverage in digital-identity contexts). Strategic: The narrative choice determines whether Digitaliseringsstyrelsen treats the project as a prudent risk-management initiative (green light for phase three) or as a political provocation (institutional resistance and stakeholder hesitation).
options: Anchor messaging in continuity-of-service risk and supplier-concentration exposure, treating geopolitical and privacy implications as secondary context, maximizing institutional receptiveness to procurement-language changes.

Lead with European digital-sovereignty framing and alignment with EU strategic autonomy objectives (citing EDIS, NIS2, and eIDAS2 resilience language), accepting political salience and US-trade-policy scrutiny in exchange for stronger alignment with government digital-strategy rhetoric.

Integrate all three narratives (continuity, sovereignty, and privacy) equally in communications and policy documents, maintaining philosophical coherence but diffusing message impact and making procurement language changes slower to achieve.
levers.review_lever: Controls narrative resonance vs. institutional resistance. Weakness: The options do not address the risk that geopolitical framing, if linked to Chinese or Russian alternative technologies, will trigger security-community backlash and vendor claims of state-sponsored favoritism, potentially delegitimizing the entire project.

levers.name: Phase-Two Demonstrator Scope Boundary and Feature Depth Versus Feasibility Evidence
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project must decide whether phase-two demonstrators are **minimal feasibility proofs** (one authentication flow, no integration with tax/banking/health portals, ~200 lines of core logic) versus **integration-ready prototypes** (multiple public-sector service integrations, compliance checklist coverage, ~2,000+ lines of production-oriented code). Systemic: Minimal-scope demonstrators finish phase two on schedule and budget (3.5 million DKK, months 9–16), reducing risk of cost overrun by 70%, but provide limited stakeholder confidence in real-world applicability (institutional conviction: 40–50%); integration-ready prototypes increase credibility substantially (institutional conviction: 75–85%) but risk scope creep, 25–35% budget overrun (800k–1.2M DKK), and 2–4 month schedule slip, threatening phase-three momentum. Strategic: This choice determines whether phase-three procurement language is supported by 'sufficient evidence of viability' or by 'proof-of-concept credibility gaps that invite incumbent skepticism.'
options: Deliver minimal-scope demonstrators (single authentication pathway, simulated public-sector identity provider, no downstream service integration) completing on schedule and budget, accepting limited institutional confidence in near-term applicability.

Extend demonstrators to include integration with at least two simulated Danish public-sector services (tax filing, health portal access) and compliance checklist alignment, accepting 25–30% budget increase and 2–3 month schedule extension for substantially higher stakeholder confidence.

Build modular demonstrators that allow phase-two completion of core authentication within budget, with extensibility hooks for phase-three partners to add service integrations post-delivery, deferring integration complexity but requiring formal commitment from downstream stakeholders (AltID, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen).
levers.review_lever: Controls demonstrator credibility vs. project budget containment. Weakness: The options assume integration with 'simulated' services is sufficient to convince procurement officers; actual vendor responses will likely demand live integration or formal certification pathways, rendering simulated integration-readiness insufficient for procurement language justification.

levers.name: European Digital Identity Wallet Alignment and Standards Influence Sequencing
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can pursue **Danish-first advocacy** (focus phase-three engagement entirely on AltID and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen), **parallel EU engagement** (simultaneously engage with European Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and standards bodies in months 15–24), or **EU-first leverage** (redirect resources toward EU-level standards bodies in months 12–16, using EU alignment as a way to pressure Danish procurement indirectly). Systemic: Danish-first approach concentrates resources and influence (80% probability of Danish AltID traction) but leaves European Digital Identity Wallet standards vulnerable to vendor-friendly language, missing a multiplier effect; parallel EU engagement adds institutional complexity and requires 200–400k DKK additional travel/coordination budget, but increases likelihood of EU-level language precedent (50–60% probability) that indirectly supports Danish procurement (compounding effect: 65–75% combined traction); EU-first redirection maximizes long-term institutional advantage (EU language influence: 70–80%) but risks Danish-level traction (AltID influence: 25–30%), a bet on long-cycle policy change rather than near-term procurement outcomes. Strategic: This choice determines whether the project is a Danish infrastructure initiative with European spillover, or a European standards campaign that uses Danish AltID as a proof-of-concept.
options: Concentrate phase-three resources on Danish procurement and AltID engagement, maintaining intensity and focus while accepting that European Digital Identity Wallet standards may not incorporate platform-neutrality language without external EU-level advocacy.

Allocate 30–40% of phase-three budget (1.0–1.4M DKK) to simultaneous engagement with European Digital Identity Wallet working groups and EU standards bodies, creating parallel pressure and allowing Danish work to anchor broader European resilience language.

Redirect core phase-three resources (months 15–20) toward EU-level standards bodies and technical working groups, positioning Danish demonstrators and feasibility work as proof-of-concept for European requirements, accepting reduced AltID engagement in exchange for EU multiplier effect.
levers.review_lever: Controls geographic scope vs. institutional depth. Weakness: The options do not account for the fact that EU-level standards work is typically multi-year (2026–2028+) and its influence on Danish procurement timelines is speculative, making EU engagement a hedge rather than a primary intervention lever for the 24-month project horizon.

responses.summary: The seven levers are well-distributed across **demonstrator credibility, institutional timing, coalition anchoring, technical architecture, narrative framing, scope management, and geographic leverage**, avoiding repetitive procurement-language levers and naming each with domain-specific precision. Strengths: levers 1, 4, and 6 directly challenge technical-versus-pragmatic trade-offs that the plan document foregrounds; levers 3 and 5 address institutional resistance and stakeholder psychology explicitly; lever 2 and 7 sequence engagement windows realistically. Critical flaw: **Missing lever on incumbent operator resistance and competitive reframing**. The plan mentions 'do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators,' but no lever explicitly addresses how the project converts or neutralizes MitID operator, app-store policy, or legacy-certification-body objections into procurement requirements. Concrete addition: Add **'MitID Operator Engagement Strategy and Competitive Threat Reframing'** to address whether the project frames fallback systems as complementary (reducing single-point-of-failure risk) or competitive (implicitly critiquing MitID), and whether phase-three procurement language is negotiated with operators as inevitable infrastructure evolution or imposed against institutional resistance.

levers.name: Feasibility Report Framing and Institutional Positioning
levers.consequences: Immediate: A published feasibility and risk report reframes platform dependency as a resilience and supplier-concentration issue rather than a purely technical or ideological concern, shifting bureaucratic attention from feature parity to contingency-planning risk. Systemic: Framing dependency as a public-sector continuity problem increases likelihood that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or AltID stakeholders formally acknowledge the risk in procurement language by 15–25%, because risk articulation is harder for institutional actors to dismiss than privacy advocacy alone. Strategic: A credible, institution-friendly risk narrative that decouples platform-neutral requirements from activist framing enables procurement teams to justify supplier-diversity language to finance and security committees without appearing to challenge Apple and Google directly.
options: Commission an independent feasibility report through a recognized Danish research institution that emphasizes supplier concentration, contingency resilience, and procurement neutrality, explicitly avoiding ideological positioning and instead anchoring the argument in public-sector business continuity and regulatory compliance under eIDAS2.

Conduct an internal Digitaliseringsstyrelsen-commissioned risk audit on MitID's platform dependencies, framed as a mandatory suppliers-risk assessment aligned with EU procurement and resilience directives, positioning the study as routine compliance work rather than external pressure.

Develop a multi-stakeholder white paper jointly authored by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the Danish Financial Supervisory Authority, and an academic security lab, presenting platform-neutral fallback access as a regulatory requirement under proposed Nordic digital identity standards, establishing shared institutional ownership.
levers.review: Controls technical credibility vs. institutional buy-in. Weakness: The options do not account for timing friction—Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may commission studies but deprioritize them unless tied explicitly to an immediate procurement or regulatory deadline.

levers.name: Demonstrator Scope and Evidence Weight
levers.consequences: Immediate: Two lean non-production demonstrators (mobile-based FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS, plus a browser-or-token-based fallback path) provide technical proof that standards-based, platform-neutral access is feasible without requiring Apple or Google as trust anchors. Systemic: Working demonstrators reduce perceived technical risk by 30–40% among procurement teams and EU standards bodies, because visual, hands-on evidence shifts confidence away from 'theoretically possible' toward 'operationally demonstrated,' even when demonstrators are not production-certified. Strategic: Demonstrators create a credible artifact that justifies inclusion of platform-neutral and fallback-access language in AltID procurement documents without forcing Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to build or certify an alternative identity system themselves.
options: Build a tightly scoped mobile demonstrator using GrapheneOS and open-source FIDO2 server infrastructure that authenticates only against a non-production Danish identity-provider simulation, explicitly documented as a proof-of-concept with no integration path to live MitID or AltID systems.

Develop a browser-based and hardware-token fallback authenticator (supporting FIDO2 USB keys and WebAuthn) that works across iOS and Android without requiring app-store distribution, paired with a minimal simulated public-sector identity-provider, demonstrating that secure authentication can operate outside platform app-distribution channels.

Create a modular reference implementation library in Go and TypeScript that third-party developers can use to prototype platform-neutral authentication patterns, released under open-source governance with explicit non-production warnings, enabling external security researchers and procurement teams to test and adapt the design without direct project involvement.
levers.review: Controls technical scope vs. institutional legitimacy. Weakness: The options underestimate the time required for security vetting and stakeholder sign-off on demonstrators; a 'reference implementation' risks becoming a de facto standard before any independent review, creating reputational exposure.

levers.name: Coalition Leadership and Stakeholder Lock-In
levers.consequences: Immediate: A formal coalition spanning privacy advocates, academic security researchers, civil-society organizations, contingency-planning experts, and sympathetic Folketinget members amplifies the project's voice beyond a single research team and distributes reputational risk across multiple credible institutions. Systemic: Coalition engagement increases the probability that at least one formal institutional stakeholder (academic institution, privacy watchdog, or parliamentary committee member) publishes or co-authors a position paper on platform-neutral access by month 12, creating independent pressure that reduces reliance on the project itself for narrative coherence. Strategic: A multi-stakeholder coalition makes it politically costly for Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to ignore platform-neutrality language, because dismissing the proposal now requires public rebuttal to multiple respected institutions rather than a single external project.
options: Establish a formal advisory board with rotating leadership from the Danish Data Protection Authority, IT-Politisk Forening, and Cøbenhavn Kommune's digital innovation office, meeting monthly and publishing position statements on platform dependency, creating institutional entrenchment and shared governance.

Recruit and financially support independent peer-review by international experts (from EU digital-identity working groups, BSI Germany, Swedish eID authorities) to publicly validate feasibility findings, generating third-party institutional endorsement that strengthens credibility with Danish authorities.

Organize a series of on-the-record workshops and policy forums with Finanstilsynet, Ministry of Health, Danish National Police, and the Danish Agency for Data Supply, positioning platform-neutral authentication as a cross-sector resilience requirement for banking, healthcare, and law enforcement, not just a general-purpose public-sector issue.
levers.review: Controls coalition autonomy vs. institutional alignment. Weakness: The options do not address the risk that coalition members may fragment or deprioritize platform-neutrality work once they face competing institutional demands, particularly if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen signals low immediate priority.

levers.name: Procurement Language and AltID Intervention Timing
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project submits formally drafted procurement requirement language (platform-neutrality, fallback-access, supplier-concentration, continuity-planning clauses) to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the AltID steering committee before month 16, embedding the language directly into official requirements documentation rather than leaving it as a recommendation. Systemic: If procurement language reaches final AltID tender documents, adoption likelihood for at least one platform-neutral or fallback requirement increases by 50–70%, because procurement teams follow explicit written requirements far more reliably than general advocacy; conversely, if procurement deadlines pass before language is formally submitted, the intervention window closes for 3–5 years. Strategic: Embedding platform-neutrality language in procurement documents institutionalizes the requirement regardless of political or vendor pushback, because procurement compliance becomes mandatory for any bidder, shifting the default from 'optional innovation' to 'contractual obligation.'
options: Submit formally drafted procurement amendments to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's AltID procurement working group by month 14, specifying that any AltID implementation must include a certified fallback authentication path independent of proprietary app-store distribution, with detailed compliance metrics and acceptance criteria.

Engage directly with the AltID technical steering committee to propose a formal 'resilience and contingency' procurement track that runs parallel to the main architecture workstream, focusing on fallback and platform-neutral authentication as a separate contractual requirement and evaluation criterion.

Wait until the official AltID Request for Proposal (RFP) public comment period and submit detailed technical comments and requirement proposals through formal public-procurement channels, ensuring that feedback is documented in the official record and requires a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
levers.review: Controls intervention timing vs. procurement lock-in risk. Weakness: The options do not account for the possibility that AltID procurement timelines may shift, procurement governance may change hands, or formal comment periods may open and close without adequate notice to the project.

levers.name: EU Standards Alignment and Extraterritorial Leverage
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project engages with European Digital Identity Wallet working groups and eIDAS2 standards bodies to advocate for platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms in EU-wide digital identity materials, positioning Denmark as a leader in interoperability and resilience rather than a lone voice. Systemic: If language on platform-neutrality or fallback access appears in official EU Digital Identity Wallet guidance by month 20, Danish procurement teams face 25–40% increased pressure to adopt similar language, because EU directives and soft-law guidance create compliance expectations that are difficult for individual member states to ignore. Strategic: EU-level standards alignment converts platform-neutrality from a Danish procurement issue into a pan-European interoperability requirement, leveraging regulatory momentum to shift vendor and incumbent behavior across multiple markets simultaneously.
options: Designate a project team member to represent Denmark in the eIDAS2 technical implementation working groups, presenting demonstrator findings and platform-neutrality requirements as contributions to the Wallet's interoperability and resilience tracks, formally submitting technical and procurement input by month 18.

Coordinate with Sweden's eID platform (BankID, Freja) and German sovereignty initiatives (BSI, gematik) to jointly publish a position paper on platform-neutral digital identity requirements, framing it as a Nordic–German resilience coalition and submitting it to relevant EU bodies and the European Commission's digital infrastructure task force.

Commission an external EU-based policy institute (such as the Centre for European Policy Studies or a relevant technical standards organization) to produce a white paper on platform dependence in European digital identity systems, releasing it through EU channels to establish an independent institutional narrative that Danish efforts can reference and amplify.
levers.review: Controls EU regulatory leverage vs. Danish sovereignty autonomy. Weakness: The options assume EU bodies will prioritize platform-neutrality concerns within existing Digital Wallet timelines; in practice, EU standards bodies often defer to market-driven approaches and move slowly on sovereignty issues.

levers.name: Regulatory Approval Pathway and Compliance Framework
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project maps and formally engages with the specific regulatory approval gates and decision-makers required for platform-neutral authentication to be recognized as compliant under eIDAS2 Substantial level, including NSIS, MitID certification bodies, and AltID's technical governance. Systemic: Securing formal written guidance from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the Danish eIDAS2 compliance authority on how platform-neutral authenticators qualify for 'Substantial' level certification by month 14 reduces regulatory uncertainty by 35–50%, because bureaucratic clarity reduces the perception of legal risk and makes procurement teams more willing to accept non-proprietary designs. Strategic: A formal certification pathway for platform-neutral fallback authenticators removes the structural argument that 'only Apple and Google can meet regulatory requirements,' shifting the debate from 'Is this even legal?' to 'Which vendor best implements the legal standard?'
options: Initiate a formal dialogue with the Danish eIDAS2 competent authority and MitID's certification body to clarify how GrapheneOS-based or hardware-token authenticators qualify for Substantial level assurance, requesting written guidance on compliance pathways and approval timelines before month 12.

Propose a formal 'Platform-Neutral Authenticator' certification scheme as an amendment to Danish NSIS or AltID governance documents, specifying technical, security, and resilience requirements that any platform-independent design must meet, creating a formal institutional track rather than relying on ad-hoc compliance interpretation.

Engage with relevant EU notified bodies (NIST, BSI, or equivalent EU regulatory bodies) to establish a pilot interoperability program for platform-neutral authenticators under eIDAS2, positioning Denmark as a test jurisdiction and creating formal regulatory precedent across Europe.
levers.review: Controls regulatory clarity vs. incumbent gatekeeping. Weakness: The options do not account for the possibility that existing regulatory bodies may be reluctant to establish new certification pathways, perceiving them as undermining established MitID or Apple/Google compliance models.

levers.name: Contingency Planning and Geopolitical Risk Framing
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project explicitly frames platform-neutral access as a contingency and resilience measure tied to potential geopolitical disruptions, supply-chain vulnerabilities, or regulatory changes affecting US technology companies (e.g., TikTok bans, sanctions, or digital sovereignty laws), reframing the issue away from ideology toward risk management. Systemic: Contingency framing increases institutional receptiveness by 20–35%, because public-sector organizations are more willing to fund resilience measures for low-probability but high-impact scenarios than to challenge existing vendors on principle; moreover, if external geopolitical events occur (US export controls, Apple policy changes), contingency language that was previously marginalized suddenly becomes immediately relevant. Strategic: A formally documented contingency plan tied to platform-neutral access ensures that if MitID or AltID dependencies become exposed through policy change or supply disruption, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has a pre-established fallback architecture and procurement roadmap, rather than scrambling to build an alternative under crisis conditions.
options: Develop a formal 'Digital Identity Continuity-of-Service Plan' for Digitaliseringsstyrelsen outlining specific failure scenarios (Apple/Google policy change, US sanctions, platform availability disruption) and mapped fallback architectures, submitted by month 12 as a risk-management document rather than a strategic proposal.

Commission a geopolitical and supply-chain risk assessment from an independent security or strategic consultancy, explicitly analyzing the exposure of Danish critical identity infrastructure to US technology policy, export controls, and platform-vendor policy changes, framing the work as mandatory due diligence.

Establish a formal 'Digital Identity Resilience Steering Committee' within the Ministry of Digitization with representatives from the Finance Ministry, Defense, and Foreign Service, tasked with reviewing supplier concentration risk across critical infrastructure and formally endorsing platform-neutral fallback requirements as a defense-policy matter, not merely a technical or convenience issue.
levers.review: Controls external risk framing vs. institutional panic resistance. Weakness: The options may inadvertently trigger defensive responses from incumbent vendors or create the perception of anti-US sentiment, potentially delegitimizing the project if contingency framing is perceived as alarmist rather than evidence-based.

levers.name: Technical Credibility Threshold and Demonstrator Ambition Calibration
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deciding whether demonstrators prove feasibility through high-fidelity simulation of live MitID endpoints or through abstract proof-of-concept on sandboxed environments determines the credibility weight the project carries into phase three. → Systemic: High-fidelity demonstrators require 6–8 additional weeks of development and 15–20% budget reallocation, but increase institutional confidence in fallback paths by an estimated 40–50% based on peer review feedback; low-fidelity approaches preserve timeline but risk being dismissed as 'not real-world relevant' by procurement decision-makers. → Strategic: This choice determines whether the project's technical output functions as a binding constraint on AltID design discussions or remains advisory commentary that can be deferred without political cost.
options: Develop demonstrators using live MitID staging environments and actual GrapheneOS device hardware, with formal security review by an independent third party before public release, to establish maximum institutional credibility even if timeline slips to month 20.

Build functionally realistic but architecturally isolated demonstrators that simulate MitID and eIDAS2 endpoints using open-source reference implementations, with security review limited to internal team and invited academic reviewers, balancing credibility against schedule.

Create proof-of-concept prototypes using simplified authentication flows and synthetic identity-provider environments, publish methodology and limitations transparently, and focus phase-three leverage on standards input rather than technical validation.
levers.review: Controls technical purity vs. institutional influence. Weakness: The options do not adequately address the risk that demonstrator complexity may alienate non-technical procurement stakeholders, nor do they specify how security review findings will be communicated to decision-makers in accessible language.

levers.name: Coalition Composition and Civil-Society Legitimacy Anchoring
levers.consequences: Immediate: Recruiting specific civil-society organisations, academic institutions, and parliamentary advocates establishes the project as a public-interest initiative rather than a vendor-neutral technical exercise. → Systemic: Including organizations with established credibility on privacy (e.g., Dansk Folkepartis digital-rights caucus, university security labs) increases the probability that phase-one findings receive parliamentary attention and media coverage by an estimated 30–35%, while also introducing competing priorities and slower decision cycles (±3–4 weeks in consensus-building). → Strategic: Early coalition lock-in shapes what language (resilience vs. privacy vs. sovereignty) dominates the public narrative, influencing whether AltID procurement teams frame platform neutrality as a security imperative or as activist pressure to be managed.
options: Invite 2–3 established civil-society organisations with existing parliamentary relationships (e.g., human-rights groups, digital-rights advocates) to co-author the phase-one feasibility report and formally endorse the project's framing, accepting slower decision cycles but gaining institutional legitimacy.

Partner selectively with academic researchers and one sympathetic Folketinget member to co-sign policy proposals, limiting coalition overhead while maintaining plausible independence from government capture.

Build coalition reactively during phase one by publishing feasibility findings first and then inviting external stakeholders to validate conclusions, preserving project agility but risking that narrative framing is already set by institutional actors.
levers.review: Controls insider legitimacy vs. grassroots momentum. Weakness: The options assume that civil-society partners will prioritize platform neutrality equally with privacy or affordability; they do not account for potential coalition fragmentation if partner organizations prioritize conflicting agendas (e.g., anti-tech regulation vs. digital-access equity).

levers.name: Regulatory Framing Architecture and Risk Narrative Construction
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project must decide whether to emphasize MitID's dependency as a supplier-concentration risk (procurement/resilience frame), a democratic-accountability issue (governance frame), or a privacy/data-minimization concern (civil-liberties frame). → Systemic: Resilience framing increases traction with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and procurement teams by ~25%, but requires quantifying continuity-of-service costs; governance framing resonates with parliamentarians but invites Teliadenmark and other incumbents to reframe as populist critique of innovation; privacy framing activates civil society but may alienate technology-optimist factions within government. Each frame carries ~2–3 week delays in stakeholder alignment because different audiences prioritize different evidence types. → Strategic: The selected narrative becomes the lens through which all subsequent demonstrator results and policy proposals are interpreted; misalignment between the project's framing and key decision-makers' preexisting priorities compounds institutional friction by month 20–24.
options: Anchor the entire project narrative on quantified continuity-of-service and resilience risk, producing a formal supplier-concentration audit showing the estimated cost-impact of a six-month iOS or Android policy change affecting MitID distribution, to align directly with public-sector enterprise-risk frameworks.

Present platform dependency as a democratic-sovereignty issue tied to Denmark's constitutional safeguards and public-sector autonomy, emphasizing that critical government services should not depend on commercial platform policies, and frame fallback paths as a constitutional resilience requirement.

Construct a three-layered narrative (resilience + governance + privacy) where each audience segment receives customized messaging, accepting the risk of perceived inconsistency but maximizing relevance across fragmented decision-making bodies and stakeholder camps.
levers.review: Controls institutional risk language vs. sovereignty rhetoric. Weakness: The options do not address the possibility that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may perceive any framing emphasizing platform dependency as implicit criticism of MitID's current design and respond defensively rather than engaging constructively with the project's proposals.

levers.name: EU Coordination Timing and Standards Influence Sequencing
