levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can engage EU-level eIDAS2 and European Digital Identity Wallet working groups either concurrently with Danish phase-two work or sequentially after Danish demonstrators are complete. → Systemic: Concurrent engagement (starting month 6) amplifies the project's voice in emerging European standards (~20% increase in language inclusion probability based on timing of EC consultation cycles) but distributes team attention and requires bilingual policy communication; sequential engagement (starting month 17) preserves focus on Danish institutional traction but risks that EU requirements have already been finalized without Danish input, and that phase-three leverage with AltID is weakened by the absence of EU-level momentum. → Strategic: EU-level adoption of platform-neutrality language creates a binding constraint on Danish AltID procurement (because eIDAS2 compliance is mandatory), whereas purely Danish advocacy can be deferred or reinterpreted by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
options: Engage the European Digital Identity Wallet working groups starting month 4 by contributing a Danish use-case paper on platform-neutral fallback paths, positioning the project as an early input to European standards architecture and influencing EC policy language before formal working drafts are locked.

Delay EU engagement until month 18, after demonstrators are complete and phase-three Danish leverage is clear, ensuring that the project's EU contribution is grounded in credible technical evidence and that Danish procurement progress can be leveraged to influence European alignment.

Establish a lightweight EU liaison role (0.3 FTE from month 6) that attends key working group meetings and informal EC consultations without committing the full project team, balancing visibility against resource constraints while maintaining optionality to escalate engagement.
levers.review: Controls early European influence vs. downstream institutional leverage. Weakness: The options underestimate the political difficulty of shifting European Digital Identity Wallet design at the working-group stage; they assume that technical input from a small Danish project will move EC policy, when in fact EC priorities are already set by member-state governments and incumbent industry coalitions.

levers.name: Demonstrator Independence and Open-Source Licensing Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can release demonstrator code under a permissive open-source license (MIT, Apache 2.0), a copyleft license (GPL v3), or under restricted institutional access with deferred public release. → Systemic: Permissive licensing accelerates adoption by downstream projects and vendors by ~40%, but relinquishes the project's narrative control and creates risk that demonstrator code is used by platforms to claim false compliance; copyleft licensing preserves some control and signals commitment to open standards, but reduces adoption by commercial entities by ~25–30% and may invite legal scrutiny from patent holders; restricted access protects institutional control and allows selective distribution for credibility building, but weakens the public-interest framing and invites accusations of gatekeeping. → Strategic: The licensing choice determines whether the project's technical work becomes a public good (enabling downstream resilience research) or an institutional asset (enabling the project to maintain influence over interpretation and deployment).
options: Publish all demonstrator code and architecture under GPL v3, requiring that derivative systems remain open-source and documenting the licensing choice as a statement of commitment to digital-commons principles and non-commercial resilience, accepting reduced vendor adoption as a trade-off for ecosystem integrity.

Release demonstrators under a permissive MIT license with explicit documentation of intended use (research and institutional validation only) and a formal statement that commercial deployment must undergo independent security audit, balancing openness with accountability.

Maintain demonstrator source code under institutional access (available to authorized academic and government partners under research agreements), publish detailed architecture and methodology publicly, and commit to full open-source release following formal European Digital Identity Wallet standards finalization, preserving leverage during the critical phase-three window.
levers.review: Controls ecosystem enablement vs. institutional positioning. Weakness: The options do not adequately address the tension between claiming that platform-neutral authentication is a public good (requiring open access) and maintaining sufficient project control to ensure that demonstrators are not misused by incumbents to create false-compliance narratives or proprietary alternatives.

levers.name: Formal Authority Engagement Strategy and Institutional Capture Boundaries
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project must define the depth of operational engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the MitID operator—ranging from advisory consultation (information-sharing, no decision influence) to co-design partnership (shared research agenda, steering-committee seats). → Systemic: Deep operational engagement (steering committee, formal input into AltID architecture) increases the probability of procurement-language inclusion by ~35% and accelerates institutional decision cycles by 8–12 weeks, but creates risk that the project becomes perceived as a government arm and loses civil-society coalition support; conversely, purely advisory engagement preserves independence and coalition credibility, but reduces institutional traction and increases the likelihood that phase-three procurement language is ignored as 'external input' without binding weight. → Strategic: The engagement model chosen determines whether the project functions as an independent credibility source influencing procurement from outside or as an insider constrained by institutional politics and incumbent resistance.
options: Establish a formal joint steering committee with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the MitID operator beginning month 3, with the project team providing research input directly into AltID architecture discussions and accepting governance-sharing obligations that may delay some decisions but embed platform-neutrality requirements into procurement planning.

Engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen through structured quarterly consultations and formal comment periods on public procurement drafts, maintaining arm's-length independence while ensuring that the project's findings reach decision-makers in documented form that creates institutional accountability.

Conduct engagement through informal meetings with sympathetic contacts within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and publish findings independently, maximizing the project's freedom to critique and propose alternatives while accepting that institutional influence depends on sustained media and parliamentary pressure rather than formal channels.
levers.review: Controls institutional integration vs. analytical independence. Weakness: The options do not address the likelihood that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen will view the project as either a compliance threat (if too independent) or as administrative overhead (if too integrated), and that the project's ability to influence procurement depends less on the chosen engagement model than on whether top political decision-makers have already committed to platform neutrality as a policy priority.

levers.name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Architecture and Technological Diversity Forcing
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project must select whether to develop a single fallback pathway (e.g., browser-based FIDO2 only) or to demonstrate multiple architecturally distinct fallback routes (browser-based, hardware-token, SMS-backed, distributed identity) to show that no single foreign platform is a technical necessity. → Systemic: A single pathway is defensible, lean, and completable within phase-two budget (~3.0 million DKK), but a procurement reviewer can argue 'this is only one option—not a systemic alternative'; multiple pathways require 4.5–5.0 million DKK (20% budget increase) and 4–6 additional weeks of integration testing, but create a more robust innovation signal and reduce the argument that fallback authentication is a niche use case. The presence of two or three distinct working pathways increases the estimated probability of AltID procurement language including 'support for multiple authentication methods' by ~30–40%. → Strategic: Multiple pathways establish that platform-neutral resilience is not a technical constraint or an ideological luxury, but a practical engineering problem with multiple solutions—fundamentally shifting the burden of proof onto incumbents to argue why diversity should be rejected.
options: Develop two distinct demonstrators in parallel: one mobile-native FIDO2/WebAuthn system on privacy-respecting Android and one browser-based authentication using standard-compliant OpenID Connect with optional hardware-token support, showing that resilience does not require proprietary platform dependency.

Create a single authoritative fallback pathway using browser-based authentication with explicit hardware-token integration, supported by a detailed technical specification showing how this approach scales to national-level deployment without requiring changes to MitID's current app-based infrastructure.

Develop a modular authentication framework that demonstrates three distinct fallback pathways (browser-based, SMS-backed OTP, hardware-token) integrated into a single codebase, allowing different public-sector agencies to select pathways matching their citizen accessibility and security requirements without lock-in to any single authentication method.
levers.review: Controls simplicity vs. systemic resilience demonstration. Weakness: The options assume that multiple demonstrators necessarily increase institutional confidence, when in fact overambition in scope can result in incomplete implementations that reduce credibility more than a single, well-executed fallback pathway would achieve.

levers.name: Demonstrator Technical Risk Positioning and Certification Pathway Selection
levers.consequences: Immediate: Choosing between lightweight proof-of-concept (GrapheneOS WebAuthn) versus production-grade hardware-backed fallback (FIDO2 + secure enclave simulation) determines whether reviewers treat the work as exploratory or as evidence of viability. Systemic: A low-certification-threshold demonstrator may reach stakeholders 6 months faster but risks dismissal as 'not production-relevant'; a high-certification-level build (80% toward formal audit readiness) delays phase-two delivery by 3–4 months but anchors claims that platform-neutral access meets 'Substantial' assurance levels—a 40–50% improvement in procurement credibility with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen. Strategic: This trade-off determines whether the project influences AltID procurement language as a credible technical peer or as an academic thought experiment.
options: Build a lightweight GrapheneOS + WebAuthn integration that proves concept feasibility within 6 months and accepts that production-grade certification is left to future operators.

Develop demonstrators with formal security review (third-party vulnerability assessment and compliance gap analysis) targeting 'Substantial' assurance compliance, accepting 3–4 month schedule friction and 400–600k DKK additional review budget.

Deliver two parallel demonstrators—one rapid proof-of-concept for stakeholder communication and a second higher-assurance build targeting formal evaluation frameworks—requiring split technical team and staged phase-two funding release.
levers.review: Controls demonstrator ambition vs. implementation velocity. Weakness: The options do not account for the institutional politics of certification: even high-assurance work may be dismissed if the certifying body (e.g., a MitID operator or legacy regulator) views it as competitive threat rather than evidence.

levers.name: AltID Engagement Timing and Procurement Document Infiltration
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can attempt 'early infiltration' by submitting written input during AltID's design or pre-procurement consultation phases (months 10–14), or 'late intervention' by targeting the formal procurement tender language itself (months 18–22). Systemic: Early engagement provides 8 months of potential design influence but depends on voluntary receptiveness; formal tender involvement guarantees visibility to procurement officers but may trigger incumbent pushback and compliance objections, adding 2–3 months of negotiation and 200–300k DKK in legal/procurement support costs. Strategic: Timing misalignment (too early = ignored, too late = entrenched resistance) determines whether platform-neutrality requirements appear as genuine design principles or as add-on clauses that vendors can legally sidestep.
options: Initiate informal engagement with AltID program management during months 9–12 by offering feasibility findings as 'best practice input,' building trust before formal procurement, and hoping voluntary alignment will emerge.

Target the formal procurement Request for Proposals (RFP) window explicitly by submitting technical requirements language as a formal stakeholder input, accepting that this surfaces the intervention as adversarial and may trigger defensive institutional responses.

Dual-track by embedding a sympathetic Digitaliseringsstyrelsen advisor or consultancy partner into early AltID working groups while separately preparing formal RFP comments, creating internal pressure for neutrality before external procurement debate occurs.
levers.review: Controls stakeholder relationship depth vs. formal procurement leverage. Weakness: The options assume AltID procurement gates and timelines are publicly known and fluid; in reality, procurement schedules often lock in months before public announcement, making timing predictions speculative.

levers.name: Coalition Institutional Embedding and Continuity-Planning Authority Leverage
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can anchor coalition membership in existing Danish institutions (university digital-governance centers, cybersecurity agencies, privacy regulators) or in new informal networks (ad-hoc working groups, civil-society consortia). Systemic: Institutionally embedded coalitions gain standing to comment on public procurement and policy consultations, raising coalition voice-amplification by 60–80% in official channels; informal networks are more agile but lack formal consultation standing and depend entirely on individual advocacy effort, reducing sustained institutional pressure by 40–50% over the 24-month project horizon. Strategic: The choice between institutional-rootedness and coalition flexibility determines whether the project's advocacy survives leadership or personnel transitions, and whether it can claim legitimate standing to influence critical-infrastructure procurement.
options: Formalize coalition participation through a steering committee hosted at a Danish university or independent research center, with official membership status for participating organizations, creating institutional permanence at cost of slower decision-making and organizational overhead.

Build an informal, fluid coalition of individual experts, researchers, and advocates who coordinate via regular working groups and social channels, maximizing agility and intellectual freedom but depending entirely on volunteer continuity and lacking formal consultation standing.

Create a hybrid structure in which a core formal steering committee (3–4 institutional anchors) is paired with an open advisory network of individual contributors, allowing official standing while preserving coalition flexibility and reducing organizational drag.
levers.review: Controls institutional credibility vs. coalition agility. Weakness: The options do not specify how to prevent institutional anchor-organizations from prioritizing their own risk-mitigation over the project's resilience advocacy, a common failure mode when formal governance adds bureaucratic caution.

levers.name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Architecture and Device-Lock Prevention
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project must choose between designing fallback systems that **assume continued mobile OS availability but reduce platform-dependency** (e.g., cross-platform WebAuthn on any OS) versus **eliminating mobile-OS requirement entirely** (browser-only, hardware tokens, or trusted-hardware-free server-side federation). Systemic: A mobile-resilient approach (reducing but not eliminating phone OS dependency) can leverage existing Android/iOS ecosystem and is familiar to certification bodies, adding 3–6 months to time-to-credibility; a fully device-lock-free design is architecturally cleaner but requires novel certification frameworks and invites 8–12 month regulatory debate over new assurance models, increasing phase-three engagement complexity by 50–70%. Strategic: This choice determines whether fallback authentication is framed as a near-term regulatory fix or as a long-term architectural redesign, affecting procurement language, certification pathways, and vendor resistance trajectories.
options: Design fallback authentication using browser-based OpenID Connect with server-side risk assessment (no app requirement), accepting that browser-only access may be perceived as weaker assurance and requires novel regulatory framing for public-sector authentication.

Develop cross-platform WebAuthn fallbacks deployable on any Android distribution (LineageOS, GrapheneOS, de-Googled variants) and FIDO2 hardware tokens, maintaining familiar mobile-app paradigm while reducing exclusive dependence on commercial platform ecosystems.

Pursue a hybrid server-side federation model (passwordless federation with risk-adaptive authentication) that eliminates device-lock-in entirely but requires substantial regulatory reframing and certification effort, delaying phase-three procurement influence by 6–9 months.
levers.review: Controls architectural purism vs. regulatory pragmatism. Weakness: The options assume fallback designs can be tested credibly without access to actual MitID backend systems or production certification infrastructure, leaving regulatory viability of any fallback pathway unvalidated until phase three.

levers.name: Geopolitical Risk Framing and Supply-Chain Continuity Narrative
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can frame platform dependency as a resilience/continuity-planning issue (focusing on supplier concentration and service-continuity risk), as a geopolitical autonomy issue (focusing on US-China tech competition and European digital sovereignty), or as a privacy/democratic-control issue (focusing on data minimization and citizen agency). Systemic: Resilience framing is most likely to resonate with risk-averse public-sector procurement officers and is defensible in EU regulation (estimated 50–60% institutional receptiveness); geopolitical framing is politically salient and aligns with EU strategic autonomy language, but triggers trade-policy scrutiny and risks being dismissed as protectionist, reducing institutional receptiveness to 30–40%; privacy framing is philosophically coherent but historically underweights procurement decisions (15–20% institutional leverage in digital-identity contexts). Strategic: The narrative choice determines whether Digitaliseringsstyrelsen treats the project as a prudent risk-management initiative (green light for phase three) or as a political provocation (institutional resistance and stakeholder hesitation).
options: Anchor messaging in continuity-of-service risk and supplier-concentration exposure, treating geopolitical and privacy implications as secondary context, maximizing institutional receptiveness to procurement-language changes.

Lead with European digital-sovereignty framing and alignment with EU strategic autonomy objectives (citing EDIS, NIS2, and eIDAS2 resilience language), accepting political salience and US-trade-policy scrutiny in exchange for stronger alignment with government digital-strategy rhetoric.

Integrate all three narratives (continuity, sovereignty, and privacy) equally in communications and policy documents, maintaining philosophical coherence but diffusing message impact and making procurement language changes slower to achieve.
levers.review: Controls narrative resonance vs. institutional resistance. Weakness: The options do not address the risk that geopolitical framing, if linked to Chinese or Russian alternative technologies, will trigger security-community backlash and vendor claims of state-sponsored favoritism, potentially delegitimizing the entire project.

levers.name: Phase-Two Demonstrator Scope Boundary and Feature Depth Versus Feasibility Evidence
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project must decide whether phase-two demonstrators are **minimal feasibility proofs** (one authentication flow, no integration with tax/banking/health portals, ~200 lines of core logic) versus **integration-ready prototypes** (multiple public-sector service integrations, compliance checklist coverage, ~2,000+ lines of production-oriented code). Systemic: Minimal-scope demonstrators finish phase two on schedule and budget (3.5 million DKK, months 9–16), reducing risk of cost overrun by 70%, but provide limited stakeholder confidence in real-world applicability (institutional conviction: 40–50%); integration-ready prototypes increase credibility substantially (institutional conviction: 75–85%) but risk scope creep, 25–35% budget overrun (800k–1.2M DKK), and 2–4 month schedule slip, threatening phase-three momentum. Strategic: This choice determines whether phase-three procurement language is supported by 'sufficient evidence of viability' or by 'proof-of-concept credibility gaps that invite incumbent skepticism.'
options: Deliver minimal-scope demonstrators (single authentication pathway, simulated public-sector identity provider, no downstream service integration) completing on schedule and budget, accepting limited institutional confidence in near-term applicability.

Extend demonstrators to include integration with at least two simulated Danish public-sector services (tax filing, health portal access) and compliance checklist alignment, accepting 25–30% budget increase and 2–3 month schedule extension for substantially higher stakeholder confidence.

Build modular demonstrators that allow phase-two completion of core authentication within budget, with extensibility hooks for phase-three partners to add service integrations post-delivery, deferring integration complexity but requiring formal commitment from downstream stakeholders (AltID, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen).
levers.review: Controls demonstrator credibility vs. project budget containment. Weakness: The options assume integration with 'simulated' services is sufficient to convince procurement officers; actual vendor responses will likely demand live integration or formal certification pathways, rendering simulated integration-readiness insufficient for procurement language justification.

levers.name: European Digital Identity Wallet Alignment and Standards Influence Sequencing
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project can pursue **Danish-first advocacy** (focus phase-three engagement entirely on AltID and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen), **parallel EU engagement** (simultaneously engage with European Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and standards bodies in months 15–24), or **EU-first leverage** (redirect resources toward EU-level standards bodies in months 12–16, using EU alignment as a way to pressure Danish procurement indirectly). Systemic: Danish-first approach concentrates resources and influence (80% probability of Danish AltID traction) but leaves European Digital Identity Wallet standards vulnerable to vendor-friendly language, missing a multiplier effect; parallel EU engagement adds institutional complexity and requires 200–400k DKK additional travel/coordination budget, but increases likelihood of EU-level language precedent (50–60% probability) that indirectly supports Danish procurement (compounding effect: 65–75% combined traction); EU-first redirection maximizes long-term institutional advantage (EU language influence: 70–80%) but risks Danish-level traction (AltID influence: 25–30%), a bet on long-cycle policy change rather than near-term procurement outcomes. Strategic: This choice determines whether the project is a Danish infrastructure initiative with European spillover, or a European standards campaign that uses Danish AltID as a proof-of-concept.
options: Concentrate phase-three resources on Danish procurement and AltID engagement, maintaining intensity and focus while accepting that European Digital Identity Wallet standards may not incorporate platform-neutrality language without external EU-level advocacy.

Allocate 30–40% of phase-three budget (1.0–1.4M DKK) to simultaneous engagement with European Digital Identity Wallet working groups and EU standards bodies, creating parallel pressure and allowing Danish work to anchor broader European resilience language.

Redirect core phase-three resources (months 15–20) toward EU-level standards bodies and technical working groups, positioning Danish demonstrators and feasibility work as proof-of-concept for European requirements, accepting reduced AltID engagement in exchange for EU multiplier effect.
levers.review: Controls geographic scope vs. institutional depth. Weakness: The options do not account for the fact that EU-level standards work is typically multi-year (2026–2028+) and its influence on Danish procurement timelines is speculative, making EU engagement a hedge rather than a primary intervention lever for the 24-month project horizon.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences, in this order):
     "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]. Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
     Both clauses are mandatory in every response, in this exact order, in one field.
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions

Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.
